spans.text: Paint one side of a rubber disk black and the other side white: stretch the disk any way you wish in three-dimensional space, subject to the condition that from any point in space, if you look down you see either the white side of the disk or nothing at all. Now make the stretched disk transparent but color its boundary black project its boundary into a plane that lies below the disk. The resulting curve is self-overlapping. We show how to test whether a given curve is self-overlapping, and how to count how many essentially different stretchings of the disk could give rise to the same curve.

Micha Sharir posed this problem at the Eighth Geometry Day at the Courant Institute. Chi-Yuan Lo suggests that its solution could be useful in processing VLSI layouts that are represented by polygon bouudaries [NSi30]. We are grateful for several discussions with Kerr Clarkson. Subhash Suri, and Bob Tarjan. Steve Fortune offered some suggestions that greatly simplified the notation (Really!). David Johnson, David Levine, and Alex Schaffer ma& helpful comments on the manuscript.
